Guide.title: Pool Maintenance Guide
Guide.subtitle: Everything you need to keep your water clear, balanced and safe.

chemistry.title: Chemistry
chemistry.intro: Three parameters keep the water balanced: alkalinity (80-120 ppm), pH (7.2-7.6) and free chlorine (1-3 ppm). Always check them in this order — alkalinity, pH, chlorine — because each one affects the next.

alkalinity.title: Alkalinity
alkalinity.p1: Alkalinity acts as a buffer for pH: as long as it stays between 80 and 120 ppm, the pH remains stable and not very volatile. In a pool, alkalinity tends to decrease over time because acidic products such as pH reducer consume the bicarbonates in the water. To bring it back into range, use sodium bicarbonate — which also slightly raises the pH.

ph.title: pH
ph.p1: pH measures the acidity of the water. A value between 7.2 and 7.6 lets every other product (chlorine, algaecide, flocculant, clarifier) work at peak efficiency. Tap water is slightly basic (pH ≈ 7.5).
ph.p2: In a pool, pH tends to rise over time due to rain and exposure to air. To lower it, use a pH reducer or muriatic acid, which barely affects alkalinity. To raise it, use sodium carbonate — though it also nudges alkalinity upward.

chlorine.title: Chlorine
chlorine.p1: Chlorine disinfects the water, preventing algae and dangerous bacteria. It comes in several forms — tablets, granules, liquid — each with a different concentration and level of stabilization.

chlorineStabilized.title: Stabilized chlorine
chlorineStabilized.p1: Trichlor tablets (typically 200 g) and 56% dichlor isocyanurate granules are both stabilized chlorines: they contain cyanuric acid that shields them from sunlight. Dichlor is very strong and acts quickly.

chlorineUnstabilized.title: Unstabilized chlorine
chlorineUnstabilized.p1: Sodium hypochlorite is a liquid chlorine at 12-15% active chlorine, with no stabilizer, used for shocks. Calcium hypochlorite is a granular form at 70-73% active chlorine, also unstabilized and suitable for shocks. The two differ in physical state (liquid/solid), concentration (~15% vs. ~73%) and pH (13 vs. 11).
chlorineUnstabilized.p2: For the same shock, calcium hypochlorite needs roughly one-fifth the weight compared to sodium. Both temporarily raise pH by about one point, because hypochlorite breaks down into hydrochloric acid (HCl) and a base; once the reaction is complete the pH returns almost to its starting point, so the net pH change after a shock is negligible.
chlorineUnstabilized.p3: ⚠️ Never mix hypochlorite with other types of chlorine: risk of explosion, fire and toxic fumes.

chloramines.title: Chloramines
chloramines.p1: When chlorine reacts with organic matter it forms chloramines (combined chlorine), which disinfect far less effectively than free chlorine. The most effective way to keep them low is simply to add more chlorine.

stabilizer.title: Stabilizer
stabilizer.p1: The stabilizer is cyanuric acid, present in trichlor tablets and in dichlor isocyanurate. It protects chlorine from UV destruction. The ideal range is 30-50 ppm: below 30 chlorine degrades too quickly in sunlight; above 50 chlorine becomes "bound" and loses effectiveness.
stabilizer.p2: Warning: repeatedly using dichlor isocyanurate for shocks rapidly raises the stabilizer level. Once it is too high, the only way to lower it is a partial drain of the pool.

hardness.title: Hardness
hardness.p1: Calcium hardness must sit between 175 and 400 ppm. Below that, water tends to corrode concrete; above it, scale forms on pipes and liner. Well-balanced hardness, together with alkalinity, helps keep pH stable.
hardness.p2: If hardness is too high, it can be treated with a sequestering agent.

algaecide.title: Algaecide
algaecide.p1: Algaecide is a preventive product that stops algae from taking hold. Specific formulations also exist for removing algae once they have appeared.

flocculant.title: Flocculant
flocculant.p1: Flocculant binds suspended particles, making them heavy enough to settle on the bottom. It is especially useful after a shock that killed off a lot of dark-green algae, when the water stays cloudy.

cleaning.title: Cleaning
cleaning.intro: The first line of defense against algae and cloudy water is constantly removing debris. The less organic food there is in the water, the less work the chlorine has to do.

circulation.title: Circulation
circulation.p1: Good water circulation is essential: the hotter it gets, the more hours the pump should run. As a general rule, 12 hours a day is a safe baseline, but the exact number depends on the pool volume and the pump's flow rate (usually expressed in gallons per hour).
circulation.p2: Temperature matters a lot: in summer you typically need roughly twice the hours compared to winter.

debris.title: Debris
debris.p1: You have to constantly remove organic and inorganic debris from the water. Organic debris (leaves, insects, pollen) feeds algae; inorganic debris (sand, dust) clouds the water. A fine-mesh skimmer net, used daily, is the first line of routine maintenance.

robot.title: Cleaning robot
robot.p1: The cleaning robot has a filter of just a few microns: it is perfect for vacuuming fine algae and particles off the bottom. For routine use, it is the most convenient way to keep the floor spotless without any manual work.

actions.title: Actions
actions.intro: Beyond day-to-day maintenance, a pool needs scheduled periodic interventions: shocks to restore the disinfectant, filter cleaning and floor cleaning.

shock.title: Shock
shock.p1: A shock consists of dumping a large amount of chlorine into the water to sterilize it and wipe out every organism. For it to work, alkalinity and pH must be in the correct range. Brushing every surface is equally important: putting algae into suspension exposes them to the chlorine.
shock.p2: In normal conditions free chlorine sits between 1 and 3 ppm; in a shock you aim for 10 ppm, and up to 30 ppm in severe cases. Calcium hypochlorite is the best choice because just a few kilograms produce a powerful shock; sodium hypochlorite also works well.
shock.p3: A shock is typically done about once a week (often less). Strong sun, heat and a heavy bather load are the factors that make it most necessary.

filterCleaning.title: Filter cleaning
filterCleaning.p1: The sand filter catches suspended particles. In normal conditions, cleaning it every 1-2 weeks is enough. After a shock, on the other hand, it should be cleaned immediately and more thoroughly — especially if the water was overrun with algae.

bottomCleaning.title: Bottom cleaning
bottomCleaning.p1: Routine floor cleaning is done with the robot. When large amounts of algae have settled on the bottom, switch to the manual vacuum with the selector set to waste, to drain the debris straight outside instead of pushing it through the filter.

formulas.title: Chlorine formulas
formulas.description: The three chlorine quantities are tied together: if you know two of them, the third one follows.

Calculations are based on the amount of active ingredient (chlorine) effectively available.

Shock.title: Pool Shock Calculator
Shock.description: Find out exactly how much chlorine to add to shock your pool.

Intro.title: How much shock does your pool need?
Intro.subtitle: 2 minutes · we guide you step by step
Intro.explanation: A shock is a big dose of chlorine that restores disinfection and wipes out algae and chloramines. This tool tells you how much product to add and which one to use — when your water actually needs it.
Intro.phWarning: First things first: the water needs balanced pH. If the pH is out of range, the shock might not work properly.
Intro.phLink: Learn how to balance the pH
Intro.callToAction: Fill in the fields below and we'll walk you through each step. 👇
Intro.revealHint: — steps appear as you fill them in —

Volume.title: How much water is in your pool?
Volume.label: Water volume
Volume.helpText: Don't know how many liters your pool holds?

Color.title: What does your water look like?
Color.subtitle: Tap the pool most similar to yours — it sets the shock intensity

Cya.title: How much cyanuric acid (CYA)?
Cya.subtitle: It's the "stabilizer" already in the water
Cya.label: Cyanuric acid
Cya.reassure: No problem: we'll use a prudent range (30–80 ppm) and the result will be a slightly wider range.
Cya.highWarning: ⚠ Your CYA is very high. Even with a shock, chlorine struggles to work. Consider partially draining and refilling your pool before shocking.
Cya.learnMore: What is it? Learn more about cyanuric acid

Chlorine.title: How much chlorine is in the water now?
Chlorine.subtitle: Fill in 2 of 3: the third is calculated for you
Chlorine.combinedNudge: Also enter combined chlorine for a more precise calculation.
Chlorine.reassure: No problem: we'll use a prudent chlorine estimate and the result will be a slightly wider range.

Result.noShockBody: Your water looks great. Just make sure combined chlorine stays below 0.5 ppm.
Result.noShockCcHighBody: Combined chlorine ({cc} ppm) is above 0.5 ppm. A breakpoint shock is recommended to burn off the chloramines:
Result.kicker: Result · how much chlorine you need
Result.explanation: Amount of chlorine needed to bring the water up to the chosen shock level.
Result.rangeWarningPure: ⚠ Average figure. Without {missing}, the pure chlorine actually needed can range from {min} to {max} {unit}. You must do a test to obtain a precise result.
Result.rangeWarningProduct: ⚠ Average figure. Without {missing}, the product actually needed can range from {min} to {max} {unit}.
Result.breakdownDose: {volume} L × ({target} − {currentFC} ppm) ÷ 1000 = {pure} g of pure chlorine
Result.breakdownDoseAssumed: {volume} L × {gap} ppm to add ÷ 1000 = {pure} g of pure chlorine
Result.winnerNoteSlam: Driven by algae severity and your stabilizer level.
Result.winnerNoteBreakpoint: Driven by your high combined chlorine (chloramines).
Result.winnerNoteFloor: Set to the minimum effective shock level for this water condition.
Result.productSection: Which chlorine do you have available?
Result.productSubtitle: Your choice changes how much product to pour in.
Result.calciumDescription: granular · raises pH · adds calcium hardness

Footer.disclaimer:
Based on the SLAM method (TroubleFreePool) and breakpoint chlorination.
Always re-test the water after circulating and follow the product's instructions. Never mix different chlorine products.

Warnings.highCya: Your CYA ({value} ppm) is very high. Consider a partial drain before shocking.
Warnings.alreadyEnough: You already have enough free chlorine for this shock level.
Warnings.lowDose: The calculated amount is very small — measuring it precisely may be hard.
